title: Rustic free-form galette
subtitle: Open shortcrust pastry tart with seasonal fruit, folded at the edges.
excerpt: A rustic galette is a free-form tart: a circle of shortcrust pastry with a fruit filling placed in the centre and the edges folded over to hold it in. There is no tin, no lining, no blind baking. It bakes directly on a tray. The result looks deliberately imperfect and tastes better than it looks.
sourceNotes: The galette is a French free-form tart tradition, distinct from a tarte (which uses a tin). The word galette refers to various flat, round baked items across French regional cooking; in patisserie it is most often a free-form pastry. The free-form approach became popular in British home baking in the 1990s and 2000s as an accessible alternative to lined tart cases, appearing in books by Nigel Slater and others. The technique is the same regardless of the fruit used; this recipe uses apples and gives seasonal alternatives.

recipe.freezeNotes: The assembled unbaked galette can be frozen on the tray. Bake from frozen at 190°C fan for 50 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry can be made and refrigerated up to two days ahead. The assembled galette can be kept refrigerated for up to four hours before baking.

recipeTools.notes: Large flat tray lined with baking paper.

content.text: A galette bakes directly on baking paper on a flat tray. The pastry is rolled into a rough circle, the fruit is piled in the middle, and the edges are folded up to form a low wall. The fold does not need to be neat; uneven pleating is part of the aesthetic.

This recipe uses apple, but it works with almost any firm fruit. Pears work in autumn. Plums work in late summer. Figs and honey work in early autumn. Berries work but release a lot of juice; add an extra tablespoon of ground almonds to the base to absorb it.

Rub {{plain-flour}}, {{icing-sugar}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and cold {{unsalted-butter}} (the pastry quantity) together until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Add 3 to 4 tablespoons of cold water, one at a time, mixing until a dough forms. Flatten into a disc, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes.
Heat the oven to 190°C fan (210°C conventional). Line a baking tray with baking paper.
Roll the pastry on a lightly floured surface to a rough circle, about 35 cm across and 3 mm thick. Transfer to the lined tray.

Scatter {{almond-flour}} over the pastry, leaving a 6 cm border. Toss {{apple}} slices with {{caster-sugar}} and {{cinnamon-ground}}, then pile them on top of the almond flour. Dot the butter over the top if using.
Fold the pastry border up and over the fruit edge, pleating as you go. Press gently to seal. Brush the pastry with beaten egg and scatter the tablespoon of caster sugar over the pastry.
Bake for 35 to 40 minutes until the pastry is deep golden and the fruit is tender and caramelised at the edges. Cool on the tray for 10 minutes before sliding onto a board to serve.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: A free-form galette is almost impossible to ruin.

items.symptom: Filling juices leaking out during baking.
items.fix: Press the folds firmly so the wall holds. A small amount of leakage is expected; the almond flour base absorbs most of it. Leaked caramel on the baking paper is not a problem.

items.cause: Too much juice from the fruit, or fruit sliced too thick.
items.fix: Slice fruit thinly. The almond flour layer absorbs excess juice; increase it to 4 tablespoons if the fruit is very juicy.
